provenance.title: Chunking Methods on Retrieval-Augmented Generation – Effectiveness Evaluation Against Computational Cost and Limitations
provenance.institution: Department of Artificial Intelligence, Faculty of Information and Communication Technology, Wrocław University of Science and Technology, Wrocław, Poland
provenance.venue: 30th International Conference on Knowledge-Based and Intelligent Information & Engineering Systems (KES 2026), Procedia Computer Science
provenance.abstract: This study is the first to systematically evaluate a wide range of chunking methods in RAG systems, emphasizing practical and methodological challenges. It shows that computationally expensive chunking methods do not yield meaningful effectiveness improvements while introducing substantially higher overhead.
key_claims: This is the first systematic evaluation comparing a wide range of chunking methods across diverse datasets and tasks.
More computationally expensive chunking methods do NOT yield meaningful effectiveness improvements over simpler baselines.
Chunking introduces practical challenges (timeouts, memory failures, implementation fragility) that are often overlooked.
Chunk quality and structural coherence are more important than chunk quantity for retrieval performance.
Fixed-size and Recursive Semantic chunking provide the best trade-off between robustness and retrieval effectiveness.

architecture.description: This is a RAG evaluation framework paper, NOT a novel model architecture paper. The system consists of a standard RAG pipeline with interchangeable chunking front-ends. The architecture is a benchmark harness, not a neural network.

components.name: Document Corpus
components.role: Input — raw documents from multiple QA datasets
components.output: Raw text D = <t1, t2, ..., tN> (sequence of tokens)

components.name: Chunker (interchangeable)
components.role: Maps document D into chunk set C = {c1, c2, ..., cm}

components.name: Embedding Model (bge-m3)
components.role: Maps each chunk c ∈ C to a dense vector v ∈ R^d

components.name: Vector Index
components.role: Stores all chunk embeddings for approximate nearest-neighbor retrieval

components.name: Retriever
components.role: Given query q, retrieves top-k chunks by cosine similarity

components.name: Reranker
components.role: Re-scores top-k retrieved chunks for improved relevance ordering

components.name: Generator LLM
components.role: Answers query conditioned on top-5 retrieved chunks (max 4000 tokens context)

components.name: LLM-as-a-Judge
components.role: Evaluates generated answers against ground-truth using 5-point Likert scale

data_flow: Chunk Set C → Embedding Model → Vector Index
Query q → Embedding Model → Query Vector E(q)
E(q) + Vector Index → Retriever → top-k chunks
top-k chunks → Reranker → reranked top-k chunks
Query + top-5 reranked chunks → Generator LLM → Answer
Answer + Ground Truth → LLM-as-a-Judge → Likert Score (1–5)
architecture.note: Architecture is a benchmark harness. No novel neural architecture is proposed.

equations.name: Document Tokenization
equations.role: Formal definition of document as a sequence of atomic token units

equations.name: Chunking Function
equations.latex: f_\theta : D \rightarrow C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_m\}, \quad c_i = \langle t_{\text{start}_i}, \ldots, t_{\text{end}_i} \rangle
equations.role: Maps document D to a set of text segments/chunks; segments may overlap
equations.note: θ represents method-specific hyperparameters (e.g. chunk size, overlap, similarity threshold)

equations.name: Top-k Retrieval via Cosine Similarity
equations.role: Core retrieval objective: select k chunks whose embeddings are most similar to query embedding

equations.name: Relevance Label Assignment
equations.role: A chunk is considered relevant if it overlaps with the extractive answer span; if no span, all chunks from the relevant document are labeled relevant

equations.name: Accuracy@k
equations.role: Fraction of queries for which at least one relevant chunk appears in the top-k retrieved set

equations.name: Recall@k
equations.role: Average fraction of all relevant chunks that are retrieved in the top-k

equations.name: Semantic Misalignment Condition
equations.latex: E(c) \text{ dominated by irrelevant content} \Rightarrow \text{semantic misalignment with } E(q)
equations.role: Theoretical motivation for chunking — poor chunking pollutes embedding space

tensor_semantics.note: This is a systems evaluation paper. No neural network weight tensors are defined. The following are the key data objects.

objects.name: document_tokens
objects.description: Sequence of N tokens in a document

objects.name: chunk
objects.description: Subsequence of document tokens; L_i varies by method

objects.name: chunk_embedding
objects.description: Dense vector representation of a chunk from bge-m3; d is the model's embedding dimension

objects.name: query_embedding
objects.description: Dense vector representation of a query from bge-m3

objects.name: retrieved_chunks
objects.description: Set of top-k chunks selected by cosine similarity; k=5 or k=10

objects.name: generation_context
objects.description: Concatenation of top-5 retrieved chunks fed to the generator LLM

training_pipeline.note: This is an evaluation/benchmark paper. There is NO model training. The evaluation process is described instead.

step1_chunking.description: Each chunking method segments all documents in each dataset
step1_chunking.parallelism: 20 nodes, each running ~1/4 of a node's resources

step2_embedding.role: Embed all chunks into dense vector space

step3_indexing.description: Store all chunk embeddings in a vector index for retrieval

experiments.name: Evidence Retrieval
experiments.description: Evaluate whether relevant chunks appear in top-k retrieved results for each query

experiments.name: End-to-End RAG Answer Generation
experiments.description: Evaluate quality of LLM-generated answers using LLM-as-a-judge on 5-point Likert scale
experiments.note: TextTiling excluded from this experiment due to modified implementation

chunking_methods_evaluated.name: TextTiling
chunking_methods_evaluated.robustness: MEDIUM — completes retrieval but excluded from end-to-end

chunking_methods_evaluated.name: GraphSeg
chunking_methods_evaluated.robustness: LOW — spaCy memory errors on large documents (S marker)

chunking_methods_evaluated.name: LumberChunker
chunking_methods_evaluated.robustness: LOW — exceeds 48h time limit on many datasets (T marker)

chunking_methods_evaluated.name: DenseX (Propositions)
chunking_methods_evaluated.robustness: LOW — very high latency; frequently times out (T marker)

failure_markers.S: spaCy library triggered error due to excessive temporary memory requirements on documents > 1M characters

key_findings: Fixed-size chunking is fastest by orders of magnitude (<1s) with competitive accuracy.
Recursive Semantic achieves highest Accuracy@5 (89.36%) with moderate time (4.90m avg).
LumberChunker achieves highest Recall@10 (78.16%) and LLM-judge score (4.35) but fails on most datasets due to timeouts.
GraphSeg achieves highest Recall@10 among robust methods (61.75%) but fails on large documents.
DenseX is the slowest method (avg 15h) with the lowest Accuracy@5 (69.10%), making it generally impractical.
Chunk quality/coherence is more important than chunk quantity for retrieval performance.
Advanced chunking methods do not meaningfully outperform simple baselines when robustness and time are considered.

implementation_assumptions.basis: Standard BAAI/bge-m3 model specification — not stated in paper

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Vector index uses cosine similarity with approximate nearest-neighbor (e.g., FAISS or similar)
implementation_assumptions.basis: Standard RAG pipeline practice; paper states cosine similarity formula but not specific index library

implementation_assumptions.assumption: PIRB evaluation uses the standard PIRB benchmark setup from Dadas et al. 2024
implementation_assumptions.basis: Cited directly; specific query/answer format inferred from citation

implementation_assumptions.assumption: TextTiling modification aligns chunk boundaries to nearest sentence boundary rather than paragraph
alternatives: Original paragraph-level alignment (produces no effective segmentation)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: LumberChunker uses GPT-OSS-20B as the internal chunking LLM (not the original GPT-4)
Hyperparameters for all methods taken from original publications or official repos; not tuned
Chunk sizes NOT normalized across methods — each method uses its own natural granularity
alternatives: Normalizing all methods to same average chunk size

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Python is the implementation language (inferred from spaCy, LangChain, and standard ML tooling)
implementation_assumptions.basis: Common practice; spaCy and LangChain are Python libraries

implementation_assumptions.assumption: For the reproducible harness, we will implement Fixed-size, Recursive Semantic, Sequential HAC, and Max-min as the four robustly-completing methods
implementation_assumptions.basis: Paper explicitly identifies these four as the methods that 'consistently complete processing across datasets'
alternatives: Include GraphSeg and TextTiling as optional extras

ambiguities.description: It is not specified whether the PIRB benchmark's queries or the dataset's own queries are used for evaluation. The paper mentions 'unified Polish Information Retrieval Benchmark (PIRB)' but also uses non-Polish datasets (SQuAD, TriviaQA, etc.).
ambiguities.primary_assumption: PIRB is used as the evaluation framework/protocol (accuracy/recall metrics), but each dataset's own queries are used as the query set

ambiguities.description: GraphSeg shows S (spaCy error) for most large datasets but reports numbers for some. The exact cutoff for document length triggering the error is not stated.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Errors occur for documents > 1,000,000 characters as stated in the marker description

ambiguities.description: GPT-OSS-20B is described as the underlying LLM for LumberChunker, but the exact API endpoint, temperature, and sampling parameters are not specified
The paper says 'a subset of the original datasets' was used for the end-to-end experiment due to 'missing answers in some datasets' but does not specify which datasets were excluded
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Datasets without ground-truth answer spans were excluded; NQ and NovelQA are likely excluded based on table
